one_line_takeaway: Few-shot demonstrations added to system-prompt jailbreak defenses reliably hurt Task-Oriented Prompts (up to -21.2% safe rate) but only inconsistently help Role-Oriented Prompts once you look past the AdvBench-only headline number to the paper's own cross-dataset average (-0.6% to -0.9%), so the practical takeaway is narrower than the abstract implies: avoid combining ToP with few-shot, but don't assume RoP+few-shot is a universal win.
key_finding: Few-shot demonstrations have opposite average effects on the two defense types on AdvBench: RoP improves with few-shot (average Delta = +2.0% for FS-General, +2.2% for FS-Harmful across non-think models; largest single case Pangu-Embedded-7B RoP 94.9%->RoP+FS-G 99.2%, +4.3%), while ToP degrades (average Delta = -6.6% for FS-General, -8.7% for FS-Harmful; largest single case Qwen-7B-Chat ToP 96.5%->ToP+FS-G 75.3%, -21.2%). However, the paper's own Table 11 cross-dataset aggregation (averaged over AdvBench, HarmBench, SG-Bench, XSTest) shows the RoP+few-shot effect is actually NOT reliably positive once all datasets are combined: RoP+FS-General = -0.6% +/- 3.1% and RoP+FS-Harmful = -0.9% +/- 5.4%, versus ToP+FS-General = -3.0% +/- 7.6% and ToP+FS-Harmful = -4.0% +/- 5.4%. So the 'RoP enhancement' headline result is driven specifically by AdvBench and is not consistent across all four benchmarks in the paper's own numbers, whereas the ToP degradation direction (negative) is consistent across the aggregated data even if its magnitude varies. Separately, on HarmBench (Table 13) RoP+FS did mostly help (e.g., Pangu-7B RoP 93.2%->RoP+FS 96.5%) while ToP+FS still hurt (Qwen-7B ToP 94.9%->ToP+FS 80.7%, -14.2%). A secondary finding: 'think' reasoning-mode model variants show lower baseline safety (e.g., Pangu-Embedded-7B-Think 75.0% vs Pangu-Embedded-7B 99.8% on direct queries), larger jailbreak-induced drops (Qwen3-8B-Think 97.3%->52.0% under attack, a 45.3pp drop), and negative few-shot interaction effects regardless of RoP or ToP.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a systematic empirical + theoretical study of how few-shot demonstrations interact with two prompt-based jailbreak defenses, Role-Oriented Prompts (RoP: 'You are a safe, helpful, and harmless AI assistant...') and Task-Oriented Prompts (ToP: 'Your task is to provide helpful, accurate, and safe responses...'). They evaluate 9 open-weight LLMs (Pangu-Embedded-1B/7B/7B-Think, Qwen-7B-Chat, Qwen3-8B/8B-Think, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, DeepSeek-7B-Chat, Llama-2-7B-Chat) across 4 safety benchmarks (AdvBench, HarmBench, SG-Bench, XSTest) and 6 jailbreak attack methods (AIM, DAN, Evil Confident, Prefix Rejection, Poems, Refusal Suppression), testing 7 defense configurations (No defense, RoP, ToP, RoP+FS-General, RoP+FS-Harmful, ToP+FS-General, ToP+FS-Harmful). They also propose a mathematical framework (Bayesian in-context-learning posterior updating for RoP; a softmax-attention-entropy/'attention dilution' bound plus an 'initial-position/attention-sink advantage' argument for ToP) intended to explain the divergent empirical pattern, and report a secondary 'think-mode paradox' finding that reasoning-enabled model variants (e.g. Qwen3-8B-Think, Pangu-Embedded-7B-Think) are more vulnerable to jailbreaks and show negative few-shot interactions under both RoP and ToP.
threat_model: Attacker sends a jailbreak-crafted user query (one of six template-based attack styles: role-play personas AIM/DAN/Evil Confident, prefix-injection forcing an affirmative opening, poetic obfuscation, or explicit refusal suppression) to an LLM that is protected only by a static system-prompt defense (RoP and/or ToP), optionally combined with 3 few-shot demonstration turns prepended to the system prompt. The defender controls only the system prompt/few-shot content, not model weights (no retraining/fine-tuning); the attacker has black-box, single-turn query access and no gradient/optimization capability (the six attacks are hand-crafted templates, not GCG/PAIR-style optimized attacks). Success for the attacker is measured as the response being judged unsafe by an external judge model.
limitations: As stated by the authors: (1) models tested are limited to 1-8B parameter open-weight models; larger models may behave differently; (2) the mechanism explanations (Bayesian posterior update, attention dilution) are hypotheses supported only by aggregate outcome correlation, not by direct attention visualization or probing -- no attention weights were actually measured; (3) only 3 few-shot examples were tested, so effects of few-shot quantity are unknown; (4) closed-source frontier models (GPT-4, Claude) were not evaluated due to access limitations, so generalization to the models most commonly deployed in production is untested. Additional issues visible from the paper's own tables not flagged by the authors: the headline 'RoP enhancement' number (+2.0% to +4.5%) is an AdvBench-specific result that does not hold up in the paper's own cross-dataset average (Table 11: -0.6% to -0.9%), which is a materially weaker and more mixed result than the abstract/intro framing suggests; the six jailbreak attacks used are all static/hand-crafted templates (no optimization-based attacks like GCG/AutoDAN/PAIR), so findings may not transfer to adaptive or optimized adversaries; safety judgments depend entirely on a single LLM judge (Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B) with no reported human validation or inter-rater agreement.

datasets_used: SG-Bench (1,442 malicious queries across 6 safety issue types)
quantitative_results: AdvBench no-attack no-defense baseline safe rate: Qwen-7B-Chat 100.0%, Pangu-Embedded-7B 99.8%, Llama-2-7B-Chat 99.8%, Qwen3-8B 99.0%, Qwen3-8B-Think 97.3%, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct 94.8%, Pangu-Embedded-7B-Think 75.0%, DeepSeek-7B-Chat 54.8%

AdvBench under jailbreak (no defense) safe rate: Llama-2-7B-Chat 86.5%, Pangu-Embedded-7B 84.3%, Qwen-7B-Chat 79.0%, Qwen3-8B 75.6%, Qwen3-8B-Think 52.0%, Pangu-Embedded-7B-Think 42.1%, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct 35.4%, DeepSeek-7B-Chat 21.7%

AdvBench RoP vs ToP defense effectiveness (No Defense -> +RoP -> +ToP): Qwen3-8B 75.6%->98.8%->93.1%; Llama-2-7B-Chat 86.5%->95.5%->92.7%; Pangu-Embedded-7B 84.3%->94.9%->96.0%; Qwen-7B-Chat 79.0%->94.6%->96.5%; Qwen3-8B-Think 52.0%->90.8%->77.4%

AdvBench few-shot interaction deltas (Table 8): Pangu-Embedded-7B RoP+FS-G +4.3%, RoP+FS-H +3.9%, ToP+FS-G -1.9%, ToP+FS-H -3.4%; Qwen3-8B RoP+FS-G +1.0%, RoP+FS-H -1.9%, ToP+FS-G +4.7%, ToP+FS-H -10.7%; Llama-2-7B-Chat RoP+FS-G -0.4%, RoP+FS-H +2.2%, ToP+FS-G -9.7%, ToP+FS-H +0.1%; Qwen-7B-Chat RoP+FS-G +0.6%, RoP+FS-H +4.5%, ToP+FS-G -21.2%, ToP+FS-H -13.6%; Qwen3-8B-Think RoP+FS-G -5.0%, RoP+FS-H -13.2%, ToP+FS-G -4.9%, ToP+FS-H -15.9%
Cross-dataset average interaction effects (Table 11, mean +/- std): RoP+FS-General -0.6% +/- 3.1%; RoP+FS-Harmful -0.9% +/- 5.4%; ToP+FS-General -3.0% +/- 7.6%; ToP+FS-Harmful -4.0% +/- 5.4%

XSTest over-refusal (Table 14, Safe Rate / Refused Rate / Gap): Pangu-7B 97.4%/78.0%/75.4%; Llama-2-7B 95.7%/83.4%/79.1%; Qwen-7B 86.9%/80.3%/67.2%; Qwen3-8B 75.3%/42.8%/18.0%; Qwen3-8B-Think 60.1%/34.5%/-5.4%
Attack-method breakdown on Pangu-Embedded-7B/AdvBench (Table 6): No Attack 99.8%, Poems 98.5%, Evil Confident 97.3%, DAN 89.2%, Refusal Suppression 85.6%, AIM 72.1%, Prefix Rejection 63.1%
baselines_compared: No-defense baseline (raw model, no system prompt defense)
RoP + FS-General (3 benign few-shot Q&A turns)
RoP + FS-Harmful (3 few-shot turns demonstrating refusal of harmful requests)
